title: Profile Display Name Controlled Expansion
briefing: The profile team wants to expand display names to 48 characters and support more common real-world name formats. The implementation is behind a feature flag and has completed its main release checks. Review the remaining evidence and decide whether the release needs to stop.

introducedIssues.summary: Manual exploratory QA did not sample every less-common supported punctuation combination, although automated contract tests cover the release boundary and representative international-name cases.

evidenceCards.title: QA Release Check
evidenceCards.body: Profile updates passed on desktop and mobile layouts. QA checked the 48-character limit, common apostrophes and hyphens, accented names, and a representative non-Latin name. The exploratory session did not manually sample every less-common punctuation combination allowed by the shared validator; those combinations remain covered by the automated validation suite.

evidenceCards.title: Shared Validation Review
evidenceCards.body: The profile form and profile API now import the same display-name contract. Database storage already supports 120 characters, so this release does not require a schema migration. The change remains behind the existing profile-name-expansion feature flag.

attachments.title: Shared display-name contract tests

evidenceCards.title: Display Name Complaint Summary
evidenceCards.body: Recent profile-name complaints are mostly from users whose longer legal names were rejected by the old 32-character limit. Support found no related profile corruption, account-access issue, or failed-update spike in the current release candidate.

evidenceCards.title: Controlled Rollout Note
evidenceCards.body: Expands profile display names to 48 characters using the shared validation contract. The existing profile-name-expansion feature flag can disable the change without a database rollback. The release dashboard already tracks profile_update_failed events and the support queue will be reviewed after rollout.

expectedFindings.summary: Manual exploratory QA did not sample every less-common supported punctuation combination, leaving a small testing coverage gap despite stronger automated validation coverage.

authoring.notes: Difficulty-1 SHIP control for proportional release judgment. One legitimate low-severity testing concern remains, while shared validation, automated boundary coverage, rollback, and production monitoring support shipping.
